subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Area, Improper Integral, and Integration by Parts
stimulus:
The graphs of the functions $f$ and $g$ are shown in the figure for $0 \leq x \leq 3$. It is known that $g(x) = \dfrac{1}{x^2}$ for $x > 0$. The region $S$ is enclosed by the graphs of $f$ and $g$ and the vertical lines $x = 1$ and $x = 3$. Selected values of $f$ are given in the table below.

| $x$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f(x)$ | $1$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ |

It is also known that $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ for $x > 0$.

parts.prompt: Find the area of the region $S$ enclosed by the graphs of $f$ and $g$ for $1 \leq x \leq 3$.

common_traps: Sign error on the antiderivative of $-\dfrac{1}{x^2}$
Arithmetic error when evaluating at the bounds

parts.prompt: Evaluate $\displaystyle\int_1^{\infty} g(x)\,dx$, or show that the integral diverges.

scoring_points.description: Correct limit notation to convert the improper integral: $\displaystyle\lim_{b \to \infty} \int_1^b \dfrac{1}{x^2}\,dx$.
common_traps: Not writing the limit — treating the improper integral as a definite integral with $\infty$ as an endpoint
Using $\ln x$ as the antiderivative of $x^{-2}$ (confusing with $x^{-1}$)
Incorrect power rule — getting $-\dfrac{1}{2x^2}$ or similar

scoring_points.description: Correct evaluation and answer: $\displaystyle\lim_{b \to \infty}\left(-\dfrac{1}{b}+1\right) = 1$. The integral converges to $1$.
common_traps: Concluding the limit is $0$ instead of $1$ (forgetting to subtract the lower bound value)
Stating the integral diverges when it converges

parts.prompt: Let $h$ be the function defined by $h(x) = x \cdot f'(x)$. Find the value of $\displaystyle\int_1^3 h(x)\,dx$.
parts.scoring_notes: $h(x) = x \cdot f'(x) = x \cdot \left(-\dfrac{1}{x^2}\right) = -\dfrac{1}{x}$, but the problem intends integration by parts. Using IBP with $u = x$ and $dv = f'(x)\,dx$, so $du = dx$ and $v = f(x)$: $\displaystyle\int_1^3 x\,f'(x)\,dx = \left[x\,f(x)\right]_1^3 - \int_1^3 f(x)\,dx = \left(3 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} - 1 \cdot 1\right) - \int_1^3 \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx = (1 - 1) - \left[\ln x\right]_1^3 = 0 - \ln 3 = -\ln 3$.

scoring_points.description: Correct integration by parts setup: $u = x$, $dv = f'(x)\,dx$, giving $\displaystyle\int_1^3 x\,f'(x)\,dx = \left[x\,f(x)\right]_1^3 - \int_1^3 f(x)\,dx$.
common_traps: Choosing $u = f'(x)$ and $dv = x\,dx$ (leads to a more complicated integral)
Not applying the boundary term correctly

scoring_points.description: Correct evaluation of the boundary term: $\left[x\,f(x)\right]_1^3 = 3\,f(3) - 1\,f(1) = 3 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} - 1 \cdot 1 = 1 - 1 = 0$.
common_traps: Failing to evaluate the boundary term at both endpoints
